text.title: STAR Method Agent Rules
text.summary: Hypothetical-block rule, pronoun-shift detection, result quantification, agent scoring prohibition, and legal constraints.

rule.statement: Block hypothetical answers. When a candidate answers with "I would..." trigger the probe: "Can you give me a specific past example of when you did this?" Do not score hypothetical answers; mark as NOT_STAR.
rule.rationale: Hypothetical answers have near-zero predictive validity per behavioral psychology research. Accepting them inflates scores for candidates who are good at reasoning about what they should do, not what they actually did.

rule.statement: Flag pronoun shifts. When an answer uses "we" throughout with no first-person Action statement, trigger the probe: "What did YOU personally do in this situation?" Mark answers as "pronoun_we_only" in transcript analysis.
rule.rationale: Team re-attribution is the most common STAR inflation path. Candidates may legitimately use "we" for shared work but must identify their specific contribution before the Action is scoreable.

rule.statement: Always close on Result. For any answer that describes actions without an outcome, use the mandatory probe: "What was the end result?" An answer without R is incomplete regardless of S/T/A quality.
rule.rationale: Result quantification is the primary defense against coaching-site rehearsed STAR answers. Specific numbers (30% reduction, $2M saved) are harder to fabricate and easier to verify in reference checks.

rule.statement: Agents must not assign numeric scores to candidate STAR responses. Agents may extract component spans, flag missing components, and quote verbatim evidence. Numeric scores are always human-assigned.
rule.rationale: LLM scoring rewards fluency and polish, systematically disadvantaging non-native speakers and neurodiverse candidates. The legal liability of automated employment scoring without bias audit is substantial.

rule.statement: Cap at one competency per question. Reject any double-barreled question ("Tell me about a time you led a team AND resolved conflict"). One question, one competency, 3-5 questions per 45-minute round.
rule.rationale: Double-barreled questions allow candidates to answer only the part they are strong on, producing incomplete evidence for both competencies simultaneously.

rule.statement: Weight question generation explicitly to avoid leadership/communication clustering. Force at least one question per round covering execution, technical depth, or delivery under constraint.
rule.rationale: LLMs default to leadership and communication questions; execution and technical depth are under-covered by default, producing STAR banks that miss critical signals for IC roles.

rule.statement: Send candidates a 1-page STAR prep brief before the interview loop. This raises the floor for non-native speakers without raising the ceiling for native-speaker candidates.
rule.rationale: STAR storytelling style disadvantages candidates unfamiliar with the format. Pre-briefing reduces communication-style bias without compromising the competency signal.

antipattern.description: Live-coaching prompts sent to candidates during a real interview.
antipattern.reason: Coaching a candidate mid-interview is equivalent to fraud — it eliminates the comparative basis for the evaluation and creates legal exposure.

text.reference: Deterministic STAR completeness checker before LLM transcript analysis.
